q: Fluid is a substance that offers permanent resistance to deforming forces.
The study of fluids comprises three main parts: statics, dynamics, and kinematics.
Fluid statics is primarily concerned with fluids in motion.
Fluid dynamics focuses on fluids at rest.
Concepts like atmospheric pressure and Pascal's law are typically covered under fluid dynamics.
Archimedes' principle is a topic that falls under fluid dynamics.
Motion of fluids has no significant applications in daily life.
Gas pipelines represent an important application of fluid statics.
Hydrodynamics is the term used for the study of liquid at rest.
Hydrostatic is the study of liquid in motion.
The current chapter is mainly concerned with hydrostatic topics.
Surface tension and capillarity are excluded from the scope of this chapter.
Students are expected to learn how to differentiate fluid flow as laminar and turbulent.
Understanding Bernoulli's principle does not include its relationship with the kinetic energy of a fluid.
Bernoulli's principle describes the relationship between pressure energy, kinetic energy, and potential energy of a fluid in turbulent flow.
Stokes' law is a concept that students are expected to understand within this chapter.
Distinguishing between cohesion and adhesion is not an expected learning outcome of this chapter.
The discussion of capillarity is an explicit learning outcome for this chapter.
All fluids are considered to be only liquids.
The volume of a gas is independent of both pressure and temperature.
Boyle's law and Charles' law are relevant to the volume of a liquid.
The volume of a liquid always depends on pressure, even if its temperature is constant.
An incompressible liquid is one whose density remains constant for any pressure, provided that the temperature is kept constant.
The path or flow of fluid particles is referred to as a streamline.
Motion of fluids is classified into three main types: laminar, turbulent, and transitional flow.
In laminar flow, fluid particles move irregularly.
Laminar flow is characterized by layers of fluid mixing with adjacent layers.
Streamlines in a steady laminar flow frequently cross each other.
In laminar flow, every fluid particle arriving at a given point has a different velocity.
Steady flow implies that fluid particles move in smooth paths without mixing.
Turbulent flow occurs when fluid particles move in smooth and predictable paths.
In turbulent flow, the direction of fluid particles remains constant.
The speed of the fluid at a point in turbulent flow changes only in magnitude, not direction.
Whirling fluid movement is a characteristic of laminar flow.
The speed of fluid in turbulent flow is constant at any given point.
Ideal laminar fluid flow assumes that the fluid is compressible.
In ideal laminar fluid flow, internal friction is a significant factor.
Viscosity is the term used for internal friction in fluids.
Ideal laminar fluid flow considers a high viscosity.
Turbulent flow is taken into account when considering ideal laminar fluid flow.
Sea breeze is presented as an example of turbulent flow.
A storm is an example of a laminar fluid flow.
An ideal fluid must exhibit turbulent flow and significant viscosity.
The density of an ideal fluid is assumed to be variable.
In a pipe with varying cross-sectional areas, the mass flowing in section 1 can be different from the mass flowing in section 2.
For an ideal fluid flowing through a pipe, the volume flow rate is the same throughout the pipe.
The equation $A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2$ is known as Bernoulli's equation.
The equation of continuity implies that the liquid entering one end of a pipe leaves from the other end with a variable volume flow rate.
If the area of a pipe increases, the speed of the fluid must also increase to maintain a constant volume flow rate.
Making the hose area smaller increases the speed of water spraying from it.
The equation of continuity for fluid flow applies to situations where the density of the fluid is not constant.
The volume flow rate is always constant in a pipe, regardless of whether the fluid is ideal or not.
The concept of 'no viscosity' is essential for the equation of continuity to be strictly applicable.
Bernoulli's equation is a fundamental principle in fluid statics.
Bernoulli's equation relates pressure, flow speed, and height for any type of fluid flow.
An ideal fluid for Bernoulli's equation must have significant internal friction.
The internal friction of fluid flow is often referred to as viscosity.
Daniel Bernoulli derived his famous equation in the 19th century.
Bernoulli's equation can be derived directly from Newton's second law of motion.
The sum of pressure energy, kinetic energy, and potential energy per unit volume remains constant for any fluid flow according to Bernoulli's theorem.
Bernoulli's theorem applies to fluids with high viscosity.
For Bernoulli's equation to hold, the fluid flow must be turbulent.
The term 'pressure energy per unit volume' is part of Bernoulli's theorem.
'Kinetic energy per unit volume' is one of the components whose sum remains constant in Bernoulli's theorem.
'Potential energy per unit volume' is a component that varies significantly in Bernoulli's theorem, while others remain constant.
Air moving above an aeroplane wing travels a shorter distance than air moving below it.
To maintain the same transit time, air above the wing must move slower than air below the wing.
High velocity of air flow along the top surface of a wing results in high pressure there.
Low velocity of air flow along the bottom surface of a wing results in low pressure there.
The lift on an aeroplane wing is primarily due to a higher pressure above the wing than below it.
Bernoulli's theorem explains the generation of lift on an aeroplane wing.
In an insect sprayer, pressing the piston causes slow-moving air at the nozzle tip.
Fast moving air at the nozzle tip of an insect sprayer creates a high-pressure zone.
The liquid in an insect sprayer container is at a lower pressure than the air at the nozzle tip.
The liquid in an insect sprayer rushes up the stem due to the low pressure above it.
The Magnus effect is a phenomenon unrelated to Bernoulli's theorem.
The Magnus effect specifically describes a non-spinning object moving through a fluid.
A spinning ball moving through air does not affect the air around it.
The Magnus effect produces a pressure difference on opposite sides of a spinning ball.
Deflection of a spinning ball is a result of uniform pressure distribution around it.
The Magnus effect was named after Daniel Bernoulli.
During cyclones, air moving above a roof typically slows down, increasing pressure.
The reduced pressure above a house roof during a cyclone is due to high air speed.
During a cyclone, the pressure inside a house becomes lower than the pressure outside.
A roof can be lifted off a house during a cyclone if the external pressure is significantly higher than the internal pressure.
Most fluids are considered ideal and offer no resistance to motion.
Viscosity is the external friction between a fluid and a solid surface.
Viscosity measures the resistance to flow caused by relative motion between fluid layers.
A fluid's thickness or stickiness can be described by its viscosity.
Fluids with strong intermolecular forces tend to have low viscosity.
Fluids with low viscosity flow easily because their molecular makeup results in high friction.
Gases exhibit viscosity, but it is typically more noticeable than in liquids under ordinary circumstances.
The viscosity of liquids generally increases rapidly with an increase in temperature.
The viscosity of gases decreases with an increase in temperature.
Upon heating, liquids flow more slowly, while gases flow more easily.
Viscosity is an extensive property, meaning it depends on the amount of matter.
The SI unit of viscosity is the poiseuille (Pl).
Newton second per metre squared (N s m$^{-2}$) is an equivalent unit to the pascal second.
Dynamic viscosity measures a fluid's resistance to flow under its own weight.
Kinematic viscosity is generally less useful than dynamic viscosity.
Kinematic viscosity measures the resistive flow of a fluid under the weight of gravity.
Newton's law of viscosity states that shear stress is inversely proportional to the velocity gradient.
The symbol $\tau$ represents the velocity gradient in Newton's law of viscosity.
The coefficient of viscosity is represented by $\eta$.
Shear stress is defined as the force per unit volume.
The velocity gradient $\frac{dv}{dy}$ refers to the change in velocity over time.
Viscosity is defined as the ratio of velocity gradient to shearing stress.
Stokes' law describes the drag force on large, irregularly shaped objects.
Stokes' law was formulated by Sir Isaac Newton in the 17th century.
The drag force in Stokes' law acts upward, resisting the fall of an object.
The formula for drag force in a viscous liquid is given by $F = 6\pi \eta r v$.
In Stokes' law, $v$ represents the initial velocity of the sphere.
Terminal velocity is a constant velocity of fall.
A simple method to measure viscosity involves dropping a large cube through the fluid.
The upward thrust on a falling sphere is due to the density of the sphere itself.
At terminal velocity, the weight of the sphere is equal to the drag force minus the upward thrust.
Surface tension is the tendency of a liquid surface to behave like a rigid solid layer.
Surface tension arises from repulsive forces between liquid molecules.
Cohesion refers to attractive forces between molecules of the same substance.
Cohesion causes water molecules to spread out, thereby reducing surface tension.
A water strider is able to sink due to the high density of its legs.
Objects like paperclips float on water because their density is lower than water.
Surface tension is defined as the force per unit area acting on a liquid surface.
The formula for surface tension is $\gamma = Fl$.
The SI unit for surface tension is the Joule per meter.
Surface tension cannot be referred to as a force because it's a tension.
Capillary action is the non-spontaneous flow of a liquid into a narrow tube.
Capillary action is solely caused by cohesive forces within the liquid.
Adhesion refers to forces between molecules of the same substance.
Cohesion and adhesion work against each other in capillary action, preventing liquid from rising.
For wicking to occur, a tube's diameter needs to be very large.
Capillary action is also known as the rise or depression of a liquid in a wide tube.
The rise of water underneath the ground is an example of evaporation.
Water rising into plant roots is an illustration of the Magnus effect.
When a glass tube is dipped in water, the water level within the tube drops below the outside level.
In capillary action, a larger bore tube will result in a higher water rise.
Liquids that 'wet' the tube are depressed within thin tubes.
Mercury is a liquid that wets glass capillary tubes.
Capillarity can occur for non-wetting liquids, but to a lesser extent.
The adhesion between glass and water causes a thick film of water to be drawn up the tube.
Water rises in a capillary tube because adhesive force is less than cohesive force.
The height to which a liquid is lifted in a capillary tube is independent of the contact angle.
The height $h$ of liquid rise in a capillary tube is directly proportional to the density of the liquid $\rho$.
The height $h$ of liquid rise in a capillary tube is inversely proportional to the radius $r$ of the tube.
